title: linelight atlas
subtitle: a tour of light, atoms, and shape
intro: This atlas catalogs the primitives behind the colors that atoms emit and the math that organizes them. It runs in five acts: the lines you can see in act I, the energy levels underneath in act II, the constants and units that measure them in act III, the geometry the model is built from in act IV, and a closing pass that retraces the path. Every quantity on every card comes from a published source — NIST, CODATA, Coxeter, Bransden-Joachain, Morse-Feshbach. Anything technical can be revealed by toggling the [i] control on the card itself.

acts.title: What you can see
acts.subtitle: elements, the colors they emit, and patterns in those colors
acts.intro: This act answers a simple question: why does each element glow its own color? You will meet five **element**s, the **spectral-line**s they emit, and the **series** those lines fall into. Hydrogen carries the cleanest pattern. The other four show what changes when there are more electrons, heavier nuclei, and stronger spin-orbit effects. Later acts open up the atom; this one stays at the surface where the light comes out.

families.title: Elements
families.intro: Strike five different gases and you get five different colors. The seed catalogs **hydrogen**, helium, neon, sodium, and mercury — the elements whose lines are stored in this project. Hydrogen has one electron and is the testbed for the Rydberg formula. Helium has a closed K-shell and was discovered in the sun before being found on earth. Neon's closed-shell ground state must be jolted by a discharge before its dense red-orange thicket appears. Sodium has one valence electron above a neon core and produces the yellow streetlight doublet. Mercury is heavy enough that relativistic effects matter; one reason it is liquid at room temperature.

families.title: Spectral lines
families.intro: A glowing gas does not emit a continuous rainbow. It emits a discrete set of wavelengths — a **spectral-line** for each allowed jump between two energy levels. The brightest UV emission from cosmic hydrogen is Lyman alpha at 121.6 nm. The red glow of star-forming nebulae is Balmer alpha at 656.3 nm. Sodium's D doublet at 589.0 and 589.6 nm is the yellow of sodium-vapor streetlights and the strongest absorption pair in the solar spectrum. Every line in the atlas carries a vacuum wavelength, an upper level, and a lower level, sourced from the NIST Atomic Spectra Database.

families.title: Series
families.intro: Lines from one element cluster into named **series**, each tied to a common lower level. Hydrogen has five: Lyman ends at n=1 in the UV, Balmer ends at n=2 and produces the visible workhorses of stellar spectroscopy, Paschen ends at n=3 in the near-IR, Brackett ends at n=4, and Pfund ends at n=5. Each series spacing follows the Rydberg formula, and each converges toward a limit at the ionization edge. Sodium's D doublet is a fine-structure pair rather than a Rydberg series — it has no closed-form limit. Mercury's intercombination group label collects spin-forbidden lines made bright by relativistic mixing.

acts.title: What's underneath
acts.subtitle: energy levels and the rules that decide which jumps are allowed
acts.intro: Each line is a jump between two bound states inside the atom. This act opens that layer. You meet **energy-level**s — the discrete rungs the electron can occupy — and the **transition-type** classification that decides whether a jump is allowed at all. Some transitions are nanosecond-fast. Others are forbidden in the leading approximation and only get a faint pathway through relativistic mixing.

families.title: Energy levels
families.intro: An atom's **energy-level**s are the discrete states an electron can occupy. Hydrogen has the cleanest ladder: E_n = -13.598 eV / n², with the ground state at n=1. Levels of equal n are degenerate to first order, then split by fine structure. The hyperfine splitting of n=1 produces the 21 cm spin-flip line that traces galactic hydrogen. Multi-electron atoms like helium, sodium, neon, and mercury use term symbols of the form **²S+¹L_J** to label coupled spin and orbital angular momentum. Schematic levels in the atlas carry only the term symbol — their eV value is not in the source file and was not invented.

families.title: Transition types
families.intro: Not every pair of levels can radiate at the same rate. A **transition-type** classifies the multipole that drives the jump. **Electric dipole** (E1) is the fastest allowed channel, with nanosecond lifetimes, and most visible atomic lines are E1. Magnetic dipole (M1) is suppressed by a factor of about (αZ)² and shows up in the 21 cm hydrogen spin-flip and the [Ne II] 12.8 μm ground-term fine-structure line. Electric quadrupole (E2) is suppressed by another small factor and drives the auroral green oxygen line. Forbidden transitions violate all three at once and only emit when spin-orbit mixing opens a side door — as in mercury's 253.65 nm line.

acts.title: The framework
acts.subtitle: the constants, units, and identities that turn observations into numbers
acts.intro: This act sets the measuring stick that every line in act I implicitly cites. You meet the **constant**s the SI fixes by definition, the **unit**s spectroscopy uses to compare a wavelength to an energy, and the **identity**s that bridge them — Planck-Einstein, the Rydberg energy, the Bohr radius, the fine-structure constant. None of these are atom-specific. They are the scaffolding that lets a 656.3 nm wavelength translate to 1.89 eV, 15233 cm⁻¹, or 0.457 PHz without losing a digit.

families.title: Constants
families.intro: The 2019 SI redefinition fixed five **constant**s exactly: the speed of light c, the Planck constant h, the elementary charge e, the Boltzmann constant k_B, and the Avogadro constant N_A. Every other constant in the atlas derives from these or carries a CODATA-2022 measured value with an uncertainty. The reduced Planck constant ℏ = h/(2π) is exact because h is exact. The fine-structure constant α ≈ 1/137.036 is a measured ratio that sets the strength of the electromagnetic interaction at low energy. The Bohr radius a₀ ≈ 0.529 Å and the Hartree E_h ≈ 27.21 eV set the natural scales of atomic structure.

families.title: Units
families.intro: Spectroscopy lives between energy and length. A **unit** lets you trade one for the other without losing track of what is exact and what is measured. The metre, second, kelvin, and mole are SI base units whose definitions tie back to the fixed constants of act III. The joule is the SI derived energy unit; the electronvolt is its atomic-friendly cousin, exact since 2019 because e is exact. The wavenumber cm⁻¹ is the spectroscopist's energy proxy via ν̃ = E/(hc). Atomic units close the loop: the Bohr radius a₀ and the Hartree E_h are length and energy units in which the hydrogen atom has unit size and unit binding.

families.title: Identities
families.intro: An **identity** is a closed-form equation that ties primitives together. The Planck-Einstein relation E = hν says a photon's energy is its frequency times h. Its wavelength form, E = hc/λ, gives the handy spectroscopist's identity E·λ = 1239.842 eV·nm. The vacuum dispersion relation λν = c follows from the definition of c. From there the derived identities follow: the Rydberg energy Ry = ½ m_e c² α² sets the binding scale of hydrogen, the Bohr radius a₀ = ℏ/(m_e c α) sets its length scale, and α = e²/(4πε₀ℏc) names the dimensionless strength of the interaction.

acts.title: The geometry layer
acts.subtitle: the shapes, coordinates, and symmetries the model is built from
acts.intro: Atomic spectra fall out of a continuous mathematical structure. This act exposes that structure. You meet the **special-function**s that solve the Schrödinger equation, the coordinate systems in which it separates, and the polytopes, lattices, tilings, curved spaces, and symmetry groups that organize the broader geometric world physics shares with mathematics. None of this is decoration — every spectral line in act I is a label on an eigenfunction in act IV.

families.title: Special functions
families.intro: A **special-function** is a named solution to a recurring differential equation. Legendre polynomials P_n solve the angular Laplace equation on the sphere, and the associated Legendre functions P_l^m give the polar part of the **spherical-harmonic** Y_l^m. The hydrogen radial wavefunction uses the generalised Laguerre polynomial L_{n-l-1}^{2l+1}. Hermite polynomials carry the harmonic-oscillator eigenstates. Bessel functions J_n solve the radial Helmholtz equation in cylindrical geometry. Gegenbauer polynomials generalise Legendre. Hypergeometric functions ₂F₁ and ₁F₁ are the umbrella classes most of these special-functions sit under.

families.title: Coordinate systems
families.intro: A **coord-system** is a labeling of space in which a differential equation may separate into one-dimensional pieces. Cartesian coordinates trivially separate the free-particle Schrödinger equation. Polar coordinates separate the 2D Helmholtz equation into a Bessel radial part times an angular phase. Spherical coordinates separate any central potential — the hydrogen atom lives here. Parabolic coordinates separate hydrogen under a uniform electric field (the Stark effect). Morse and Feshbach catalogued eleven 3D systems in which the Helmholtz equation R-separates; the atlas walks through the principal ones.

families.title: Polytopes
families.intro: A **polytope** is the higher-dimensional cousin of a polygon. The atlas walks through the regular polytopes by dimension. In 2D the regular polygons run from the triangle to the dodecagon and converge to a circle in the limit. In 3D the five Platonic solids — tetrahedron, cube, octahedron, dodecahedron, icosahedron — are paired by duality. In 4D there are six regular polytopes, including the self-dual 24-cell that has no 3D analogue. Three parametric families — the n-simplex, the n-cube, and the n-cross-polytope — extend to every dimension.

families.title: Lattices
families.intro: A **lattice** is a discrete set of points repeating by translation. In 2D there are exactly five Bravais lattices — oblique, rectangular, centered rectangular, square, and hexagonal — distinguished by the symmetry of their unit cell. In 3D the count rises to fourteen, sorted into seven crystal systems by axis lengths and angles. The atlas walks through them in order from cubic to triclinic, following the International Tables for Crystallography. Each carries a conventional cell, a point group, and a Bravais class.

families.title: Tilings
families.intro: A **tiling** is a covering of a space by repeating shapes without gaps or overlaps. In the Euclidean plane there are exactly three regular tilings: triangular {3,6}, square {4,4}, and hexagonal {6,3}. The square tiling is self-dual. The triangular and hexagonal tilings are duals of each other. Drop the flatness condition and the count explodes — the hyperbolic plane admits infinitely many regular tilings, of which the atlas shows three iconic ones on the Poincaré disk. In 3D Euclidean space the cubic honeycomb {4,3,4} is the only regular tiling and is self-dual.

families.title: Curved spaces
families.intro: A **curved-space** is a Riemannian manifold whose Gaussian curvature is constant. Three families exhaust the homogeneous, isotropic cases. Euclidean spaces E^n have curvature K = 0 and are the default ambient setting of non-relativistic physics. Spherical spaces S^n have K = +1 — the eigenfunctions of the Laplace-Beltrami operator on S² are the spherical harmonics that decorate atomic orbitals. Hyperbolic spaces H^n have K = -1, the home of the hyperbolic tilings of the previous section. The atlas walks them in dimension order.

families.title: Symmetry groups
families.intro: A **symmetry-group** is the set of transformations that leave a structure unchanged. The 3D point groups in Schoenflies notation label molecular symmetries: C₂ᵥ for water, C₃ᵥ for ammonia, the cubic and octahedral groups for the high-symmetry Platonic solids. Each carries an order and contains the discrete operations of a finite molecule. The classical Lie groups extend the idea to continuous symmetries, labeled by Dynkin diagrams in the A, B, C, D series. Five exceptional series — G₂, F₄, E₆, E₇, E₈ — close the catalogue.

acts.title: Coda
acts.subtitle: how to audit any of this
acts.intro: The last act has no families. It is a closing pass that retraces the path and points at the trail of citations the atlas leaves behind. Every value on every card was either fixed by SI definition, measured by CODATA, tabulated by NIST, or derived from a closed-form identity already in the atlas. If a value is missing, it is missing because the source did not carry it.

closing.title: What you've seen
closing.intro: Act I showed five elements, their lines, and the series those lines fall into. Act II opened the atom: discrete energy levels with term symbols, and the multipole rules that decide which jumps are bright and which are forbidden. Act III handed you the measuring stick: SI-fixed constants, the units that trade energy for length, and the closed-form identities that connect them. Act IV stepped sideways into the math: special functions, separable coordinates, polytopes, lattices, tilings, curved spaces, and symmetry groups. Every quantity carries its source — toggle [i] on any card to see it.
